executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

National Medicaid enrollment stands at 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with widespread enrollment declines following the end of pandemic-era continuous coverage protections. Most major states are experiencing year-over-year enrollment decreases, ranging from modest declines in New York (-1.8%) to more substantial reductions in Michigan (-7.6%) and Illinois (-6.2%). Texas, with 4.03 million enrollees, posted a -3.1% decline that mirrors Pennsylvania's experience and falls within the typical range observed across peer states. Notably, only North Carolina among the top ten states showed enrollment growth (+0.5%), suggesting most states have completed their post-pandemic eligibility redeterminations. Total national Medicaid spending reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a national average per-enrollee cost of $2,869. Significant variation exists across states, with Pennsylvania ($4,377 per enrollee) and Massachusetts ($4,201) representing high-cost states, while Florida demonstrates notably efficient spending at $2,031 per enrollee.

Quality performance remains uneven across state programs, with a national average score of 65.6 out of 100 points. Ten states achieved high-performer status (≥75 points), led by Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80), while fifteen states scored below 60 points, with Mississippi (48) and Oklahoma (51) at the bottom. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a score of 62, placing it in the moderate performance tier but below the national average and significantly trailing top-performing states. The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% exceeds the national average of 83.2%, covering nearly 3.9 million beneficiaries, which positions Texas well for coordinated care delivery improvements. Leadership should prioritize quality improvement initiatives, particularly given Texas's substantial enrollment base and the demonstrated performance gap with leading states, while monitoring ongoing federal regulatory developments around home and community-based services quality measures that may impact future program requirements.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a more modest 3.1% year-over-year decline compared to steeper drops in peer states like Illinois (-6.2%) and Michigan (-7.6%). However, the state's performance reveals concerning gaps relative to national benchmarks. At $2,988 per enrollee, Texas spends 4.2% above the national average of $2,868, yet significantly trails expansion states like New York ($3,431) and Pennsylvania ($4,377). Among southern non-expansion peers, Texas outspends Florida ($2,031) and North Carolina ($2,616) but delivers middling results. The state's quality score of 62/100 ranks 30th nationally—below the 65.6 national average and notably behind expansion peer North Carolina, though ahead of traditional southern laggards like Arkansas (54) and Mississippi (48).

Texas demonstrates operational strength through its robust managed care infrastructure, with 93.3% penetration rates well above the 83.2% national average, suggesting effective care coordination capabilities. Yet this managed care advantage hasn't translated into quality improvements, highlighting potential gaps in provider networks, care management protocols, or outcome measurement systems. The combination of above-average spending with below-average quality outcomes indicates efficiency challenges that warrant close monitoring, particularly as the state navigates post-pandemic enrollment normalization. Key areas to watch include whether Texas can leverage its managed care platform to improve quality metrics and whether spending patterns will align more closely with peer non-expansion states while maintaining adequate access to care for its large and diverse Medicaid population.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 significantly exceeds the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost efficiency issues.

alerts.title: 15 States Show Poor Quality Performance (<60 Score)
alerts.description: Nearly one-third of states are underperforming on quality measures with scores below 60, led by Mississippi at 48.

alerts.title: Florida: Low Spending with Below-Average Quality
alerts.description: Florida combines the lowest per-enrollee spending ($2,031) among major states but lacks corresponding quality data for assessment.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Excellence: High Spending with Top Quality
alerts.description: Massachusetts demonstrates effective resource utilization with highest quality score (81) and correspondingly high per-enrollee spending ($4,201).

executiveInsights.title: Texas ranks #30 in quality with 62/100 score - improvement opportunity
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scored 62/100 on quality measures, ranking 30th out of 50 states in moderate tier. This is 3.6 points below national average of 65.6.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality scores directly impact federal funding and contract renewals. HHSC needs targeted interventions to move from moderate to high performer tier.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement strategy to HHSC leadership

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% YoY while NC grew 0.5% - trend analysis needed
executiveInsights.summary: Texas lost 3.1% of Medicaid enrollees year-over-year while comparable state NC actually grew 0.5%. Texas has 4.03M enrollees vs national decline trend.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines may signal redetermination issues or access barriers that could impact contract performance metrics and HHSC's federal compliance.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment retention strategies with HHSC eligibility team

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS quality measures for 2028 published - compliance prep required
executiveInsights.summary: CMS published new Home and Community-Based Services quality measure set for 2028 implementation. This affects managed care quality reporting requirements.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration means most enrollees will be impacted by new HCBS measures, requiring system updates and provider training.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review new HCBS measures against current Texas managed care contracts

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee cost $2,988 vs national average $2,869 - cost efficiency gap
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee, which is $119 above the national average of $2,869. Total Texas expenditures are $12.05B.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs combined with declining enrollment suggests potential inefficiencies that HHSC and managed care plans need to address for sustainability.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag cost benchmarking analysis to HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: HHS OIG active Medicaid oversight signals potential compliance reviews
executiveInsights.summary: HHS OIG shows active Medicaid portfolio oversight with ongoing work plan items. Multiple regulatory signals indicate heightened federal scrutiny.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Increased OIG activity combined with Texas's moderate quality performance could trigger compliance reviews or audits affecting contract deliverables.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review current compliance documentation with HHSC legal team

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with a 62/100 quality score, creating vulnerability in performance-based contracts and federal scrutiny.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement roadmap targeting specific CMS measures where Texas underperforms.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY, indicating potential budget pressures and operational efficiency demands.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position cost optimization and administrative efficiency solutions to help HHSC manage declining enrollment impacts.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: With 93.3% managed care penetration and $12B in expenditures, potential MCO contract renewals represent major procurement opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor Texas HHSC procurement portal for managed care RFPs and prepare comprehensive proposal capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS compliance mandate
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set creates implementation and compliance requirements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop HCBS quality measurement and reporting solutions ahead of 2028 implementation deadline.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight exposure
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid oversight docket creates audit risk but also opportunities for compliance and integrity solutions.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring and fraud prevention capabilities to address potential OIG findings proactively.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee versus national average of $2,869, indicating potential pressure for cost containment initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop analytics-driven cost management solutions to help Texas optimize per-member spending.

riskOpportunity.label: Federal data collection expansion
riskOpportunity.description: New CMS generic information collection activities for Medicaid and CHIP create additional reporting and compliance requirements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Build automated data collection and reporting capabilities to support expanded federal requirements efficiently.

riskOpportunity.label: Technology modernization wave
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple states' procurement activities visible on portals suggest broader Medicaid technology modernization trend creating competitive opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Accelerate development of next-generation Medicaid platform capabilities to capture modernization opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid serves 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: 12-Mo Enrollment Delta
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, consistent with national unwinding trends.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee annually, below the national average of $2,869.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees, well above the national average of 83.2%.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a quality score of 62, below the national average of 65.6.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active Texas procurement portals tracked: HHSC Procurement Opportunities and Electronic State Business Daily.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Six federal regulatory and OIG items tracked affecting Medicaid programs nationwide.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific Texas Medicaid milestone dates provided in current dataset.
